titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Daniel Pinello Interviews on Same-sex Marriage
titlestmt.author: Camila Zorrilla Tessler

controlnote.p: This collection contains 55 published digital objects.

did.unittitle: Daniel Pinello interviews on same-sex marriage
did.abstract: The Daniel Pinello interviews on same-sex marriage consist of oral histories (recordings and transcripts) that primarily focus on two series of events: First, the municipal issuance of marriage licenses in 2004 to lesbian and gay pairs in San Francisco, California, Sandoval County, New Mexico, and Multnomah County (Portland), Oregon, together with the statewide grant of such permits in Massachusetts. Second, the effects of Super-DOMAs (Defense of Marriage Acts) in Georgia, Michigan, North Carolina, Ohio, Texas, and Wisconsin. Super-DOMAs prohibited all forms of relationship recognition for same-sex couples and were ratified in 20 American states between 2000 and 2012. They were overturned when same-sex marriage was federally legalized in 2015.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Gift of Daniel R. Pinello, 2015 and 2023.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The materials are open to research unless otherwise noted.
Restricted fragile material. Access copies of digital files may be requested. Consult Public Services for further information.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Daniel R. Pinello graduated from Williams College with a B.A. in 1972, obtained a law degree from New York University in 1975 and a Ph.D. in political science from Yale University in 1991. He is a professor at John Jay College of Criminal Justice of the City University of New York and has written extensively on gay rights and the law.

The interviews in this collection stem from that focus, dealing especially with the impact on same-sex couples of Super-DOMA laws (Defense of Marriage Acts), or legislation that denied all rights of marriage to same-sex couples that existed in specific states previous to the federal right to same-sex marriage.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Daniel Pinello interviews on same-sex marriage consist of oral histories (recordings and transcripts) that primarily focus on two series of events: First, the municipal issuance of marriage licenses in 2004 to lesbian and gay pairs in San Francisco, California, Sandoval County, New Mexico, and Multnomah County (Portland), Oregon, together with the statewide grant of such permits in Massachusetts. Second, the effects of Super-DOMAs (Defense of Marriage Acts) in Georgia, Michigan, North Carolina, Ohio, Texas, and Wisconsin. Super-DOMAs prohibited all forms of relationship recognition for same-sex couples and were ratified in 20 American states between 2000 and 2012. They were overturned when same-sex marriage was federally legalized in 2015. These interviews were conducted by Daniel Pinello, a professor at John Jay College of Criminal Justice of the City University of New York, between 2004 and 2013. Pinello carried out the videotaped conversations on location in the ten states as part of his research for two books: "America's Struggle for Same-Sex Marriage," published in 2006, and "America's War on Same-Sex Couples and their Families, And How the Courts Rescued Them," released in 2017.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: The Daniel Pinello interviews on same-sex marriage collection is arranged chronologically.

altformavail.head: Existence and Location of Copies
altformavail.p: The entire collection is available in digital form through the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Collections are processed to a variety of levels, depending on the work necessary to make them usable, their perceived research value, the availability of staff, competing priorities, and whether or not further accruals are expected. The library attempts to provide a basic level of preservation and access for all collections, and does more extensive processing of higher priority collections as time and resources permit.
Descriptions of interviews recommended by Daniel R. Pinello were provided by Pinello.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Jeff Atwell and Gordon Fluke arrived at the Sandoval County Clerk's Office in the afternoon of the one day marriage licenses were issued to gay and lesbian pairs. In a long line for two hours, they were the third couple waiting to be processed when the operation was shut down. Although never married, the people caught in this timing mishap became known in New Mexico as "the engaged."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Gregory Gomes and Craig Holmes were the first male couple to apply for a marriage license on the single day such New Mexico pairs could do so. They were married shortly thereafter at the office of Basic Rights New Mexico in a room filled with total strangers.

scopecontent.p: Barbara Busch and Jaye Jensen, a mixed-race pair, discuss similarities and differences between the African-American civil rights movement and same-sex marriage, as well as how their own families responded to the issue.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Gary Chalmers and Richard Linnell, named plaintiffs in the Goodridge case, inventory their participation in the groundbreaking litigation and its emotional dynamics, especially with regard to protecting their minor daughter from hostile public reaction.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Douglas Johnstone, the Clerk of Provincetown, discusses the ground-level implementation of the Goodridge decision in clerks' offices, describing how he handled innumerable inquiries and applications from same-sex couples both within and outside of Massachusetts.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Ronald Crews, the president of the Massachusetts Family Institute, conveys the extensive legal and political planning that opponents of same-sex marriage went through before and after the Goodridge case. He also articulates fears of anti-religious backlash if such marriages became widespread in the U.S.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Arline Isaacson, the long-time co-chair of the Massachusetts Gay and Lesbian Political Caucus, provides ample history of how the gay community struggled unsuccessfully for years to persuade the state legislature to authorize domestic partnerships for same-sex pairs. In addition, she catalogs how hostile political forces sought to place anti-gay referenda on general-election ballots there.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Molly McKay, a founder of Marriage Equality California and Associate Executive Director of Equality California, recounts the California Supreme Court's intervention to stop San Francisco's issuance of marriage licenses to lesbian and gay pairs. She also chronicles the passage of Proposition 22 in 2000, a citizen initiative limiting marriage to opposite-sex couples.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Sharon Howell, after 25 years at Oakland University, a public institution north of Detroit, was a tenured full professor. Oakland provides affordable employee housing near campus. Howell and her substantially younger partner of 15 years made an offer to purchase a house there and secured a mortgage. Then Oakland informed Howell that, because of the state Super-DOMA, the partner would have no right to remain in the home if Howell pre-deceased her.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Scott Dennis and James Etzkorn survey the political landscape leading to passage of Michigan's Proposal 2 of 2004, a Super-DOMA limiting all legally recognized relationship rights to opposite-sex couples. They also served as plaintiffs in a lawsuit by the American Civil Liberties Union against the referendum.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Kelly Maxwell adopted a son, but Michigan doesn't permit second-parent adoptions for same-sex pairs. So her partner Rose changed her surname to Kelly's, and every six months, they have to sign a document for Rose's temporary guardianship of the son. All of this is in the hope that Rose wouldn't be questioned if she had to take him to a hospital.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Craig Kukuk and A.T. Miller (an undergraduate-program director at the University of Michigan) share that an external executive-search agency hired by the University solicited nine people in senior positions elsewhere in the country who responded up front, "I won't work in a state that treats gay couples the way Michigan does." Kukuk and Miller themselves later relocated to Cornell University in upstate New York.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Vielka Holness and Judy Keenan met in Michigan, where the latter was raised and lived for 35 years. Holness practiced law in the Wolverine State and has close ties there. Then the couple relocated to New York, Holness's home state. The University of Michigan offered her a tenure-track professorship at the same time the Super-DOMA there was adopted. As a consequence, Holness had to refuse the highly coveted position in higher education.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Lorie Burch, a Dallas lawyer with a healthcare power of attorney from her partner, describes how, during the wife's surgery under anesthesia, the doctor sought out the patient's parents for permission to conduct a biopsy, even though Burch had the legal authority to make that decision.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Anthony Cuevas and Leo Cusimano explain how, despite the state Super-DOMA, the Texas Child Protective Services agency actively recruited lesbian and gay pairs to adopt sibling groups and children with special needs, when opposite-sex couples refused to do so.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Lupe Guitiérrez and David Lawson describe several ways in which they gamed the Texas Super-DOMA. The first ploy was to have just the name of one of them on the deed to their Dallas house, while only the name of the other was on their country home, thereby securing homestead property-tax exemptions on both (which would not have been allowed if their relationship were legally recognized).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Robert Klebba and David Waugh played the system under the Super-DOMA. One qualified as a destitute farmer and therefore received Wisconsin's excellent Badger Care health insurance, designed for low-income working people. But had their personal relationship been legally recognized, no such coverage would have occurred.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: John Pepple, with a master's degree in education, followed his partner of 12 years around the country for four career moves and eventually landed in Madison. When the husband terminated their relationship, Pepple ended up with no legal rights to their home and personal property. Rather, he was entirely dependent on his former partner's largesse.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Michelle Suverkrubbe demonstrates a tragic outcome of Super-DOMAs. Her partner was the birth mother of a daughter they raised together for three years. Then the partner ended the relationship and ultimately denied Suverkrubbe any further contact with their child.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Janice Coulter and Tonda Taylor share how good friends, practicing law in Oklahoma, planned to retire in North Carolina, where one grew up and the other has close ties. However, the 2012 Super-DOMA changed those intentions.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Garrett Hall and Zachary Howell, highly accomplished professionals living in New York City in their twenties, were both raised and educated in North Carolina. They explain why the state's Super-DOMA reduced the probability of their living again in North Carolina from 100 percent to zero.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Raelene Freitag and LisaAnn Trembath, after living in Wisconsin for 20 years, relocated to Delaware. Their 2011 interview and 2013 telephone conversation describe the extended soul searching they went through as a result of the passage of the Badger State's Super-DOMA.
